forms.what: Comprehensive annual report: audited financials (income statement, balance sheet, cash flows), MD&A, business description, risk factors, properties, legal proceedings, executive compensation, and internal controls assessment.
forms.when: 60 days (large accelerated), 75 days (accelerated), or 90 days (non-accelerated) after fiscal year-end.
forms.who: All domestic public companies registered under Exchange Act Sections 12 or 15(d).
forms.why_use_it: Gold standard of financial disclosure. Every revenue, EPS, margin, and debt metric on Yahoo Finance / Finviz / Macrotrends flows from 10-K data. Without it you cannot build a proper DCF, compare historical financials, or verify audited results.

forms.full_name: Annual Report ΓÇô Foreign Private Issuers
forms.what: Foreign equivalent of 10-K. Audited annual financials under IFRS (or home-country GAAP with US GAAP reconciliation), MD&A, business description, and risk factors. Used by all ADR companies.
forms.when: Within 120 days (4 months) after fiscal year-end.
forms.who: Foreign private issuers (FPIs) listed on US exchanges.
forms.why_use_it: Only audited source for ADR stock financials. Needed to fairly compare ASML, Alibaba, Toyota, Samsung ADRs against US peers. Sites like Stockanalysis.com pull ADR data directly from 20-F filings.

forms.what: Annual report for Canadian companies using the Multijurisdictional Disclosure System. Allows use of Canadian disclosure documents accepted by SEC.
forms.when: Same day as the issuer's annual report is due to be filed with Canadian securities regulators.
forms.who: Qualifying Canadian companies listed on US exchanges under MJDS.
forms.why_use_it: Source of annual financials for major Canadian companies trading on US exchanges (e.g., Shopify, TD Bank, Canadian Natural Resources).

forms.what: Annual report filed by foreign governments and their agencies that issue securities in the US (e.g., sovereign bonds). Includes macroeconomic data, debt levels, and government finances.
forms.when: Typically within 9 months after fiscal year-end.
forms.who: Foreign governments, central banks, and government agencies that have issued securities in US markets.
forms.why_use_it: Used by sovereign bond investors and EM (emerging market) debt analysts to assess country credit risk, GDP, debt-to-GDP ratios, and fiscal position.

forms.full_name: Annual Report ΓÇô Employee Stock Plans
forms.what: Annual report specifically for employee stock purchase plans, 401(k) plans, or savings plans that invest in company securities. Contains audited plan financials.
forms.when: Within 90 days after plan's fiscal year-end (180 days for plans subject to ERISA).
forms.who: Companies whose employee benefit/savings plans hold company securities registered under the Securities Act.
forms.why_use_it: Used in equity compensation analysis. Reveals actual shares held by employees in company stock, a proxy for internal confidence in the stock.

forms.what: Unaudited quarterly financial statements for Q1, Q2, Q3. Includes balance sheet, income statement, cash flow statement, MD&A, and updates on material developments. Q4 is covered by 10-K.
forms.when: 40 days (large accelerated/accelerated) or 45 days (non-accelerated) after each of first three fiscal quarter-ends.
forms.who: All domestic Exchange Act reporting companies.
forms.why_use_it: Powers all quarterly financial tracking ΓÇö revenue growth, EPS beat/miss, margin trends, cash burn. Every earnings tracker and stock screener (Yahoo Finance, Finviz, Macrotrends) sources quarterly data from 10-Q. Most frequently used periodic filing for active investors.

forms.what: Reports material corporate events across 33 categories: earnings releases (Exhibit 99.1), M&A announcements, CEO/CFO changes, bankruptcy filings, dividend declarations, stock buyback programs, credit agreement amendments, debt offerings, SEC investigation notices, and more.
forms.when: Within 4 business days of the triggering event.
forms.who: All domestic Exchange Act reporting companies.
forms.why_use_it: The most market-moving filing. Earnings announcements, M&A deals, management changes ΓÇö all hit EDGAR as 8-Ks first. Every news feed on Yahoo Finance, MarketBeat, Seeking Alpha, and TipRanks parses 8-K filings in real time. Missing 8-K monitoring means hearing news hours or days late.

forms.what: Foreign private issuers' equivalent of the 8-K. Reports material information that the FPI disclosed or was required to disclose in its home country or on any foreign stock exchange.
forms.when: Promptly after information is made public in home country.
forms.who: Foreign private issuers registered under Section 12 of the Exchange Act.
forms.why_use_it: The only way to track material news from ADR companies ΓÇö earnings results from ASML, Shell, Alibaba, Sony all arrive via 6-K. Any platform covering international stocks monitors 6-K filings.

forms.full_name: Current Report ΓÇô Regulation A Issuers
forms.what: Current report for companies that went public via Regulation A offering (Tier 2). Discloses material events similar to an 8-K but for smaller Reg A companies.
forms.when: Within 4 business days of triggering event.
forms.who: Companies with ongoing Reg A Tier 2 offerings or reporting obligations.
forms.why_use_it: Tracks material news for smaller, non-NYSE/NASDAQ companies that used Reg A mini-IPO route. Niche but important for micro-cap and retail crowdfunding investors.

forms.what: Primary registration for domestic companies going public (IPO) or registering a new class of securities. Contains: business overview, financials (3 years audited), risk factors, use of proceeds, management, and underwriter info.
forms.when: Filed before IPO; effective after SEC review. Must be public at least 15 days before roadshow begins.
forms.who: Domestic companies conducting first public offering.
forms.why_use_it: The IPO document. Contains all historical financials and business narrative for a pre-IPO company. Every IPO-focused tool on Yahoo Finance, Seeking Alpha, and TipRanks links to the S-1 as the definitive source. Essential reading before investing in any new listing.

forms.what: Allows seasoned companies to register large amounts of securities (stock, bonds, warrants) in advance and offer them over 3 years using short-form prospectus supplements. Enables rapid capital markets access.
forms.when: Effective immediately for WKSIs; others require SEC review.
forms.who: Large public companies with at least 12 months of Exchange Act reporting.
forms.why_use_it: Dilution signal. Every S-3 filing means the company can issue new shares at will. ATM (at-the-market) programs are shelf offerings. MarketBeat and Seeking Alpha flag S-3 filings as share overhang risk. Essential for monitoring dilution exposure.

forms.what: Registers securities issued as consideration in mergers, acquisitions, and exchange offers. Contains the joint proxy statement/prospectus ΓÇö the definitive M&A voting document.
forms.when: Filed in connection with the deal; effective before shareholder vote.
forms.who: Acquirer companies using stock consideration in M&A.
forms.why_use_it: The M&A bible. Contains audited target financials, fairness opinion, pro-forma combined financials, synergy assumptions, and exchange ratio. Seeking Alpha and TipRanks M&A analysis all reference S-4 data. Critical for merger arbitrage modeling.

forms.what: Registers securities issued to employees under stock option plans, RSU plans, ESPPs, and other employee benefit plans. Automatically effective upon filing.
forms.when: Effective immediately upon filing.
forms.who: Public companies registering equity compensation plan shares.
forms.why_use_it: Every time a company files an S-8, it's registering shares for employee compensation ΓÇö a form of ongoing dilution. Platforms track S-8 filings to update the fully diluted share count used in market cap calculations.

forms.full_name: Post-Effective Amendment ΓÇô Employee Benefit Plan
forms.what: Post-effective amendment to an S-8 to update information about an employee benefit plan.
forms.when: Filed as needed after S-8 effectiveness.
forms.who: Same company that filed original S-8.
forms.why_use_it: Tracks updates to equity compensation structures ΓÇö changes to option pool size, new plan adoptions.

forms.what: Registration statement for REITs and companies primarily engaged in real estate operations. Similar to S-1 but tailored for real estate entities and their unique financial metrics.
forms.when: Filed before public offering; effective upon SEC declaration.
forms.who: REITs and real estate companies offering securities.
forms.why_use_it: Primary registration document for REIT IPOs. Contains property portfolio details, FFO projections, occupancy rates, and lease expirations ΓÇö all critical REIT-specific metrics.

forms.what: Adds securities to a prior S-4 registration in a business combination.
forms.when: Filed as needed in connection with the business combination.
forms.who: Companies issuing additional shares in M&A transactions.
forms.why_use_it: Signals expanded share issuance in M&A ΓÇö may affect the exchange ratio calculation and deal economics.

forms.what: Optional registration form for small businesses (revenue under $25M) to register securities for public sale. Discontinued in 2008.
forms.when: Discontinued Feb 2008. Historical filings only.
forms.who: Small businesses (pre-2008).
forms.why_use_it: Historical IPO database entry point for small-cap stocks that went public before 2008.

forms.full_name: IPO Registration ΓÇô Foreign Private Issuers
forms.what: Primary registration statement for foreign private issuers conducting a US IPO. Foreign equivalent of S-1. May use IFRS financials.
forms.when: Filed before US IPO; effective upon SEC declaration.
forms.who: Foreign companies listing on US exchanges for the first time.
forms.why_use_it: The foreign IPO document. Used for major foreign tech IPOs (Alibaba F-1 is one of the most read filings in history). Every international IPO tracker and ADR database sources data from F-1 filings.

forms.full_name: Shelf Registration ΓÇô Foreign Private Issuers
forms.what: Shelf registration for seasoned foreign private issuers. Allows rapid secondary offerings and debt issuances over 3 years.
forms.when: Effective immediately for WKSIs; others require SEC review.
forms.who: Seasoned FPIs with at least 12 months of Exchange Act reporting.
forms.why_use_it: Dilution monitoring for ADR stocks. Same function as S-3 but for foreign companies. Tracked when large ADR issuers raise capital in US markets.

forms.full_name: Business Combination Registration ΓÇô Foreign Issuers
forms.what: Registration of securities issued by foreign private issuers in business combinations, mergers, and exchange offers.
forms.when: Filed in connection with transaction; effective before shareholder vote.
forms.who: Foreign private issuers using stock consideration in M&A.
forms.why_use_it: Used in cross-border M&A where a foreign company acquires a US company (or vice versa) using shares. Contains pro-forma combined financials for cross-border deal analysis.

forms.what: Registers American Depositary Receipts (ADRs) representing securities of foreign private issuers. Each ADR corresponds to a specific number of underlying foreign shares.
forms.when: Filed before ADR program commences.
forms.who: Depositary banks (e.g., JPMorgan, Citibank) establishing ADR programs.
forms.why_use_it: Establishes the legal framework for ADR trading. The F-6 contains the ADR ratio (e.g., 1 ADR = 10 ordinary shares) ΓÇö critical for calculating true per-share metrics.

forms.what: Registration for Canadian issuers offering securities to existing US shareholders via rights offering.
forms.when: Filed before rights offering commences.
forms.who: Canadian issuers conducting rights offerings to US shareholders.
forms.why_use_it: Rights offerings create arbitrage opportunities between the subscription price and market price. Used by event-driven investors.

forms.what: Registration for securities of Canadian issuers issued in exchange offers or business combinations with other Canadian companies.
forms.when: Filed before the exchange offer commences.
forms.who: Canadian issuers in exchange offer transactions.
forms.why_use_it: Cross-border M&A tracking tool for Canadian company deals involving US-listed shares.

forms.what: Registration of investment-grade debt securities by Canadian issuers under MJDS.
forms.when: Filed before the offering.
forms.who: Canadian issuers registering investment-grade debt in US markets.
forms.why_use_it: Tracks Canadian corporate bond issuances in US markets ΓÇö relevant for fixed income investors monitoring Canadian corporate credit.

forms.what: General securities registration for Canadian issuers under MJDS. Similar to S-1 but allows use of Canadian disclosure documents.
forms.when: Filed before public offering.
forms.who: Canadian companies registering securities (equity or debt) in US markets.
forms.why_use_it: Tracks Canadian company capital market activities in the US ΓÇö used by cross-border portfolio managers.

forms.what: Initial registration of a class of securities on a national securities exchange (NYSE, NASDAQ) under Section 12(b). The form that makes a company an Exchange Act reporting company when listing on a major exchange.
forms.when: Filed before or upon listing on a national exchange.
forms.who: Companies listing on national exchanges that were not previously SEC reporting companies.
forms.why_use_it: Marks the moment a company becomes a full SEC reporting issuer ΓÇö often filed by direct listings, spin-offs, or emerging companies becoming exchange-listed.

forms.what: Registration of a class of securities under Section 12(g) by companies with 2,000+ shareholders (or 500 non-accredited investors) and total assets exceeding $10 million that are not exchange-listed. Triggers Exchange Act reporting obligations.
forms.when: Filed when shareholder thresholds are crossed.
forms.who: Companies crossing Section 12(g) registration thresholds.
forms.why_use_it: Tracks companies that become public reporters without doing a traditional IPO. Important for OTC market research and tracking companies that grow into SEC reporting requirements.

forms.what: Short-form registration of a class of securities under Section 12(b) for companies already registered under the Exchange Act that list on a national exchange.
forms.when: Filed when securities are listed on a national exchange.
forms.who: Already-reporting companies listing a new class of securities on an exchange.
forms.why_use_it: Tracks when a company lists new securities classes (e.g., preferred stock) on an exchange ΓÇö important for preferred stock investors.

forms.what: Notification that a successor issuer's securities are deemed registered under Section 12(g) following a corporate succession.
forms.when: Filed in connection with succession event.
forms.who: Successor companies in mergers or restructurings.
forms.why_use_it: Corporate identity tracking ΓÇö ensures continuity of reporting obligations after corporate restructurings.

forms.what: Registration of securities of foreign governments or their agencies under Section 12(b).
forms.when: Before listing on a US exchange.
forms.who: Foreign governments issuing exchange-listed securities in the US.
forms.why_use_it: Relevant for sovereign bond investors tracking government-issued securities listed on US exchanges.

forms.what: The final official proxy statement sent to shareholders before annual/special meetings. Contains: director nominations, executive compensation tables (CEO/CFO total comp), Say-on-Pay vote, auditor ratification, shareholder proposals, related-party transactions, and equity plan approvals.
forms.when: Mailed or made available at least 40 calendar days before the annual meeting.
forms.who: All public companies holding shareholder meetings.
forms.why_use_it: The definitive source for executive pay, board governance, and shareholder voting. CEO compensation data on Simply Wall St, MarketBeat governance scores, and ISS/Glass Lewis proxy recommendations all derive from DEF 14A. Must-read for ESG analysis and governance risk assessment.

forms.what: Definitive proxy statement filed by a party other than management ΓÇö typically an activist shareholder running a competing slate of directors.
forms.when: Before the shareholder meeting.
forms.who: Non-management parties soliciting proxies (activists, dissident shareholders).
forms.why_use_it: The activist's own proxy ΓÇö presents the dissident case for change. Often proposes new directors, demands strategic review, or pushes for sale of the company.

forms.what: Filed by large shareholders (owning over $5M in company securities) who communicate with other shareholders about proxy votes without formally soliciting proxies ΓÇö exempt from full proxy rules.
forms.when: Promptly when exempt soliciting materials are distributed.
forms.who: Large shareholders ($5M+) communicating about proxy issues.
forms.why_use_it: Tracks major institutional views on contested governance issues without full proxy filing overhead. Shows which large shareholders are taking public positions on proxy votes.

forms.what: Final prospectus including the public offering price, filed for IPOs and secondary offerings. The document delivered to all investors at the time of pricing.
forms.when: Filed within 2 business days of pricing.
forms.who: All companies pricing a registered public offering.
forms.why_use_it: The definitive IPO/offering document distributed to investors. Contains final price, final number of shares sold, use of proceeds, and underwriter discounts. The permanent record of every public offering.

forms.what: Written communications relating to business combinations (mergers, tender offers) filed during the solicitation period. Includes investor presentations, press releases, and factual business information distributed about the deal.
forms.when: Filed on the date the communication is made.
forms.who: Companies and advisers communicating about pending M&A transactions.
forms.why_use_it: M&A deal marketing materials ΓÇö investor presentations arguing why the deal creates value. Used by merger arbitrageurs to track deal narrative, management guidance, and deal progress.

forms.what: Final prospectus for an open-end investment company (mutual fund). Contains the fund's investment objective, fees, performance history, and portfolio manager details.
forms.when: Filed within 5 business days after fund effectiveness.
forms.who: Open-end management investment companies (mutual funds).
forms.why_use_it: The investor disclosure document for mutual funds. Fee comparison, performance track record, and manager tenure all found here. Used by fund research platforms like Morningstar.

forms.what: Condensed summary prospectus for mutual funds ΓÇö a shorter, more investor-friendly version covering key fund facts: fees, performance, risks.
forms.when: Filed simultaneously with or before the full 497 prospectus.
forms.who: Mutual funds providing a summary prospectus to investors.
forms.why_use_it: The key facts document that fund comparison sites display ΓÇö expense ratio, fund objective, performance, and risk summary all derived from 497K.

forms.what: Reports every change in an insider's holdings: open-market purchases, open-market sales, option grants and exercises, RSU vesting, gifts, and other transactions. The most frequently filed Section 16 form.
forms.when: Within 2 business days of the transaction.
forms.who: All corporate insiders: officers, directors, 10%+ shareholders.
forms.why_use_it: The single most-tracked insider signal. Finviz, Yahoo Finance, MarketBeat, and TipRanks all prominently display Form 4 insider buys/sells. Cluster buying (multiple insiders buying simultaneously) is statistically one of the strongest long-term buy signals. Academic studies consistently show insider purchase portfolios outperform.

forms.what: Discloses 5%+ ownership by any investor with activist intentions ΓÇö plans to influence management, push strategic changes, seek board seats, or acquire control. Must describe the purpose of the investment in detail. Item 4 (Purpose of Transaction) is the most important section.
forms.when: Within 5 business days of crossing the 5% threshold.
forms.who: Any investor or group acquiring 5%+ beneficial ownership with activist intent.
forms.why_use_it: One of the most powerful stock catalysts on EDGAR. 13D filings from Elliott, Icahn, ValueAct, or Third Point cause immediate 5ΓÇô20% price spikes. Every stock news platform headlines 13D filings as breaking news. Event-driven investors front-run activist campaigns by monitoring 13D filings in real time.

forms.what: Discloses the complete US equity portfolio of institutional investment managers with $100M+ in US equities. Shows every position: issuer name, CUSIP, share class, number of shares, market value, and investment discretion. Filed quarterly.
forms.when: Within 45 days after each calendar quarter-end (February 14, May 15, August 14, November 14).
forms.who: Investment managers exercising investment discretion over $100M+ in US equity securities.
forms.why_use_it: The most analyzed quarterly filing in equity markets. Powers 'copycat investing' ΓÇö tracking Berkshire, Bridgewater, Tiger Global, and thousands of hedge funds. Institutional flow data drives Yahoo Finance Holders tab, Finviz, Wisesheets, TipRanks Smart Portfolio. 13F analysis is an entire investment industry.

forms.what: Filed when a manager required to file 13F has no holdings to report ΓÇö all positions were liquidated or transferred.
forms.when: Same deadline as 13F-HR: within 45 days of quarter-end.
forms.who: Institutional managers with 13F obligations but zero reportable holdings.
forms.why_use_it: Signals when a major fund has completely exited all US equity positions ΓÇö rare but significant. Can signal fund closure, strategy change, or transition to non-reportable assets.

forms.what: Amendment to a previously filed 13F-HR. Filed when a manager discovers errors in reported holdings.
forms.when: As needed after original 13F-HR.
forms.who: Institutional managers correcting 13F-HR filings.
forms.why_use_it: Corrects institutional ownership data ΓÇö important for databases tracking precise fund position sizes and changes over time.

forms.what: Filed when a third party launches a formal tender offer to purchase another company's shares directly from shareholders at a specified premium price. Includes offer price, conditions, financing, minimum shares needed, and plans for target post-acquisition.
forms.when: Filed on the date the tender offer commences.
forms.who: Third-party companies or investors making tender offers.
forms.why_use_it: Acquisition announcement document. Tender offers typically offer 20ΓÇô50% premiums ΓÇö immediate price catalyst. Yahoo Finance, MarketBeat, and Seeking Alpha headline SC TO-T filings as M&A breaking news. Merger arb funds analyze offer conditions and bump probability from this filing.

forms.what: Amendment to update terms of a pending tender offer ΓÇö typically to extend the deadline, increase the offer price, waive conditions, or respond to competing bids.
forms.when: As material changes occur during the offer period.
forms.who: Third-party offerors amending their tender offer.
forms.why_use_it: Offer bump tracker. SC TO-T/A increasing the price is a direct arbitrage opportunity signal. Extension amendments signal regulatory delays. Price bumps after SC TO-T/A are among the cleanest merger arb setups.

forms.what: Filed when a company makes a tender offer to repurchase its own shares at a premium to market price. A more aggressive form of share buyback than open-market repurchase programs.
forms.when: Filed when the self-tender commences.
forms.who: Public companies buying back their own shares via formal tender offer.
forms.why_use_it: Premium buyback signal ΓÇö management is so confident the stock is undervalued they're offering a premium to buy it back. Strong capital return and undervaluation signal tracked by MarketBeat and Yahoo Finance.

forms.what: Amendment to an issuer's self-tender offer, updating terms, price, or deadline.
forms.when: As material changes occur during self-tender.
forms.who: Companies amending their self-tender offers.
forms.why_use_it: Tracks changes to buyback tender terms ΓÇö price increases are additional positive capital return signals.

forms.what: Written communications about a tender offer made before or during the offer period ΓÇö investor presentations, press releases, and deal-related communications.
forms.when: Filed on the date the communication is made.
forms.who: Offerors making pre-offer or during-offer written communications.
forms.why_use_it: M&A deal communications tracking. Shows how management and acquirer are framing the deal narrative to shareholders ΓÇö used in contested bid situations to track changing deal rationale.

forms.what: Used for tender offers, exchange offers, rights offerings, and business combinations where the target is a foreign private issuer with less than 10% US shareholders. Allows use of home-country documents.
forms.when: Filed at commencement of the offer.
forms.who: Parties in cross-border offers where the target FPI has minimal US shareholder base.
forms.why_use_it: Tracks cross-border M&A deals where the target is a foreign company. Used by international merger arbitrage strategies.

forms.what: Required for any transaction by a public company or its affiliates that would result in the company being deregistered. Requires detailed fairness analysis, financial projections, and justification that the transaction is fair to unaffiliated shareholders. The most shareholder-protective filing requirement.
forms.when: Filed before the going-private transaction is sent to shareholders.
forms.who: Controlling shareholders, management teams, or companies in take-private transactions.
forms.why_use_it: Minority shareholder protection document. The financial projections disclosed here often reveal management's true view of company value ΓÇö sometimes far higher than the take-private offer. Activist investors and litigation lawyers scrutinize 13E-3 filings intensively.

forms.what: Filed by the national securities exchange notifying the SEC that it is delisting a security. The exchange's official notice that a stock is being removed from its listing.
forms.when: Filed when the exchange delists the security.
forms.who: National securities exchanges (NYSE, NASDAQ, NYSE American) delisting a security.
forms.why_use_it: Official delisting confirmation. After a Form 25 is filed, the stock typically moves to OTC markets or ceases trading entirely. The most definitive delisting signal in EDGAR.

forms.what: Offering statement for Regulation A mini-IPOs. Tier 1 allows up to $20M; Tier 2 allows up to $75M per 12-month period from non-accredited investors. Requires an offering circular similar to a simplified prospectus. Qualified by SEC (not declared effective).
forms.when: Filed before commencing offering; qualified by SEC (typically 60ΓÇô90 day review).
forms.who: US or Canadian companies conducting Regulation A mini-public offerings.
forms.why_use_it: Mini-IPO tracking. Companies too small for traditional NASDAQ/NYSE IPO use Reg A. Popular on retail investment platforms like Republic, Wefunder, and StartEngine. Creates early-stage investment opportunities before possible uplist to major exchanges.

forms.what: Offering statement for Regulation Crowdfunding (Reg CF) transactions through SEC-registered funding portals. Allows companies to raise up to $5M from the general public.
forms.when: Filed with SEC before offering commences.
forms.who: US companies raising money through Reg CF via registered crowdfunding portals (Wefunder, Republic, StartEngine).
forms.why_use_it: Crowdfunding offering disclosure. Contains business plan, financials, and use of proceeds for companies raising from retail crowdfunding investors. Used by portals to display investment opportunity details.

forms.full_name: Notice of Exempt Offering ΓÇô Regulation D
forms.what: Notice of a private securities offering conducted under Regulation D exemptions (Rules 504, 506(b), 506(c)). Not a registration ΓÇö just a notice to the SEC that a private offering is happening. Used for EVERY venture capital, private equity, and hedge fund raise.
forms.when: Within 15 calendar days after the first sale of securities.
forms.who: Companies, funds, and startups raising money under Regulation D from accredited investors.
forms.why_use_it: The primary signal of private capital raises. EDGAR Form D filings reveal: which companies are raising, how much, at what price, and who is managing the deal. Venture capital databases and private market trackers (Pitchbook, Crunchbase) supplement their data with Form D disclosures.

forms.what: Amendment to update a previously filed Form D. Required for material changes to offering terms and annually for ongoing Reg D offerings.
forms.when: Within 15 days of material change; annually within 15 days of one-year anniversary.
forms.who: Companies and funds that filed the original Form D.
forms.why_use_it: Tracks ongoing private capital raise activity ΓÇö total raised, new investors, changed terms. Venture investors track D/A amendments to follow fundraising progress of portfolio companies.

forms.what: Registration statement for open-end management investment companies (mutual funds). Includes the full prospectus (fees, investment objective, risks, performance) and Statement of Additional Information (SAI).
forms.when: Initial registration before offering shares; annual post-effective amendments.
forms.who: Open-end management investment companies (mutual funds) offering shares to the public.
forms.why_use_it: The definitive disclosure document for every US mutual fund. Morningstar, Yahoo Finance, and fund research platforms derive expense ratios, investment mandates, and manager information directly from N-1A filings.

forms.what: Registration statement for closed-end funds conducting public offerings. Closed-end funds trade on exchanges unlike open-end mutual funds.
forms.when: Filed before offering shares; effective upon SEC declaration.
forms.who: Closed-end management investment companies.
forms.why_use_it: Source of investment mandate, fees, and leverage details for closed-end funds (CEFs). CEF-specific metrics like discount/premium to NAV require accurate N-2 data.

forms.what: Registration for securities issued by investment companies in fund mergers, reorganizations, or exchange offers between funds. Discloses the rationale and terms of the fund merger.
forms.when: Filed before the fund merger; effective upon SEC declaration.
forms.who: Registered investment companies merging with other funds.
forms.why_use_it: Fund merger impact analysis. When two mutual funds merge, the N-14 explains what investors get ΓÇö new fund name, new expense ratio, new investment mandate. Morningstar tracks all fund mergers via N-14 filings.

forms.full_name: Annual Census Report ΓÇô Investment Companies
forms.what: Annual census report for registered investment companies providing operational data about service providers, fund characteristics, and certain financial metrics.
forms.when: Within 75 days after fund fiscal year-end.
forms.who: All registered investment companies (mutual funds, ETFs, closed-end funds, UITs).
forms.why_use_it: Operational fund data used by fund databases for administrator, custodian, and auditor tracking. Used by compliance and due-diligence databases.

forms.what: Certified annual and semi-annual reports for management investment companies, including full audited financial statements and portfolio holdings.
forms.when: Within 10 days after transmittal to shareholders.
forms.who: Management investment companies (mutual funds, ETFs, closed-end funds).
forms.why_use_it: Full fund portfolio holdings with audited financials. More detailed than N-PORT and includes manager commentary. Used for detailed fund analysis and attribution.

forms.what: Semi-annual version of N-CSR providing mid-year unaudited portfolio and financial data.
forms.when: Within 10 days after transmittal to shareholders for semi-annual period.
forms.who: Management investment companies.
forms.why_use_it: Mid-year portfolio update for fund analysis. Shows fund holdings changes at the half-year mark between annual reports.

forms.what: Monthly report disclosing complete portfolio holdings of registered investment companies. Includes every position's value, risk metrics, and derivative exposures.
forms.when: Within 30 days after month-end; public disclosure delayed 60 days.
forms.who: Registered management investment companies with assets over $1B.
forms.why_use_it: Most granular fund holdings data available. More frequent and more detailed than 13F ΓÇö includes fixed income, derivatives, and international positions. Used by institutional research for fund flow analysis.

forms.what: The portfolio holdings exhibit filed as part of the N-PORT monthly filing. Contains the actual position-level data.
forms.when: Filed as part of monthly N-PORT submission.
forms.who: Same as NPORT-P filers.
forms.why_use_it: The underlying data table behind the N-PORT filing ΓÇö useful for quantitative analysts pulling raw fund position data.

forms.what: Monthly schedule of portfolio holdings of money market funds. Discloses every instrument held including issuer, maturity, yield, and market value.
forms.when: Filed monthly; within 5 business days after month-end.
forms.who: Money market funds registered under the Investment Company Act.
forms.why_use_it: Money market safety monitoring tool. During financial stress, investors and regulators monitor N-MFP filings to track money market fund exposure to specific issuers ΓÇö the 2008 'breaking the buck' scenario.

forms.full_name: Proxy Voting Record ΓÇô Investment Companies
forms.what: Annual report disclosing how a mutual fund voted on every proxy proposal at every company it holds. Required for full voting transparency.
forms.when: Filed annually by August 31 for votes cast during the 12 months ending June 30.
forms.who: All registered management investment companies.
forms.why_use_it: Shows how major fund families (BlackRock, Vanguard, Fidelity) voted on executive comp, board elections, and ESG proposals. Used by governance researchers to evaluate fund voting behavior.

forms.what: Quarterly schedule of portfolio holdings for management investment companies. Replaced by N-PORT in 2019.
forms.when: Discontinued; replaced by N-PORT as of 2019.
forms.who: Replaced by N-PORT filings.
forms.why_use_it: Historical quarterly holdings data for funds pre-2019. Important for backtesting fund strategy changes over time.

forms.what: Annual and semi-annual reports sent to investment company shareholders ΓÇö the fund's investor communication document.
forms.when: Filed within 60 days after transmittal to shareholders.
forms.who: Management investment companies.
forms.why_use_it: Fund shareholder communication. Contains manager's letter, performance review, and market commentary ΓÇö the portfolio manager's perspective on markets and holdings.

forms.what: Initial notification filing to register as an investment company under the Investment Company Act of 1940.
forms.when: Before beginning operations as a registered investment company.
forms.who: New investment companies registering under the Investment Company Act.
forms.why_use_it: Marks the legal formation of a new registered investment company. Used to track new fund launches.

forms.what: Automatic post-effective amendment to a mutual fund's registration statement for routine updates ΓÇö annual updates to performance data, updated prospectus, new share class additions.
forms.when: Within 60 days of fiscal year-end; automatically effective under Rule 485(b).
forms.who: Mutual funds updating their registration statement/prospectus annually.
forms.why_use_it: Annual fund data refresh. Contains updated performance track record, current expense ratios, and any new share classes ΓÇö the source for annual mutual fund database updates.

forms.what: Post-effective amendment requiring SEC review (non-automatic). Used when material changes require SEC approval before taking effect.
forms.when: Filed 60 days before desired effectiveness; requires SEC review.
forms.who: Mutual funds making material changes to investment policies or operations.
forms.why_use_it: Material fund change alert ΓÇö investment mandate shifts, fundamental policy changes, or new fund strategies require 485APOS. Important for tracking when a fund drifts from its stated objective.

forms.what: Registration form for SEC-registered investment advisers. Contains: business description, clients and AUM, fee schedules, ownership structure, disciplinary history, and conflicts of interest. Also serves as the annual updating amendment (Part 1 filed with SEC; Part 2 ΓÇö brochure ΓÇö provided to clients).
forms.when: Initial: before commencing advisory activities. Annual update: within 90 days of fiscal year-end.
forms.who: Investment advisers with $110M+ AUM (advisers with $100M+ may also register voluntarily).
forms.why_use_it: The definitive disclosure for every major hedge fund, mutual fund manager, and RIA. AUM data, fee structures, key personnel, and disciplinary history all derive from ADV filings. Used by institutional due diligence teams and RIA databases like SEC Investment Adviser Search.

forms.what: Filed when an investment adviser withdraws its SEC registration ΓÇö ceasing operations, falling below registration threshold, or transitioning to state registration.
forms.when: Within 180 days of ceasing to qualify for SEC registration.
forms.who: Investment advisers withdrawing from SEC registration.
forms.why_use_it: Tracks fund/adviser closures. When a hedge fund files ADV-W, it signals fund shutdown ΓÇö relevant for investors in or tracking those strategies.

forms.what: Systemic risk report for private funds (hedge funds, private equity, venture capital) managed by SEC-registered advisers. Provides gross/net AUM, leverage ratios, liquidity profiles, and counterparty exposure.
forms.when: Large hedge fund advisers ($1.5B+): quarterly within 60 days + 72-hour event reporting. Large private equity advisers ($2B+): quarterly event-based reports within 60 days. Large liquidity fund advisers ($1B+): quarterly within 15 days. Others: annually within 120 days.
forms.who: SEC-registered advisers managing private funds with at least $150M in private fund assets.
forms.why_use_it: Systemic risk monitoring. Form PF data (in aggregate) is used by regulators to monitor systemic risk in private markets. Individual filings reveal fund-level leverage, strategy type, and liquidity mismatches ΓÇö used by institutional counterparties and researchers.

forms.what: Semi-annual and annual report by ABS securitizers disclosing repurchase demand history ΓÇö instances where investors demanded the securitizer buy back a loan due to representation and warranty breaches.
forms.when: Semi-annually (45 days after June 30) and annually (90 days after December 31).
forms.who: Securitizers of asset-backed securities that are Exchange Act reporting companies.
forms.why_use_it: ABS credit quality tracker. High repurchase demand rates indicate poor loan underwriting quality ΓÇö a leading indicator of ABS pool deterioration used by structured finance analysts.

forms.what: Standardized electronic asset-level data file for asset-backed securities, providing loan-by-loan detail: original balance, current balance, delinquency status, geographic data.
forms.when: Filed at time of ABS registration and with each periodic report.
forms.who: Issuers of registered asset-backed securities (mortgage-backed, auto loan, credit card ABS).
forms.why_use_it: Loan-level ABS data ΓÇö the raw data used by structured finance quants to model pool performance, prepayment speeds, and loss projections. Essential for MBS/ABS research.

forms.what: Monthly or periodic distribution report for asset-backed securities issuers. Reports payments made to ABS investors, pool balances, delinquencies, and defaults.
forms.when: Monthly or per the ABS deal's distribution schedule.
forms.who: Issuers of asset-backed securities registered under the Exchange Act.
forms.why_use_it: ABS performance tracker ΓÇö shows actual cash flows, pool factor, and default rates for specific ABS deals. Used by fixed income investors monitoring their ABS/MBS portfolio performance.

forms.what: Annual report for foreign governments and their agencies that issue securities in US markets. Covers macroeconomic conditions, fiscal accounts, government debt levels, and balance of payments.
forms.when: Typically within 9 months after fiscal year-end.
forms.who: Foreign governments, central banks, and government agencies with US-listed securities.
forms.why_use_it: Sovereign credit analysis. Emerging market bond investors use 18-K filings to assess country fundamentals: GDP growth, inflation, primary fiscal balance, and debt sustainability metrics.

forms.what: Annual report filed by multilateral development banks (World Bank, IDB, ADB) and similar institutions that issue bonds in US markets.
forms.when: Annually per the institution's reporting schedule.
forms.who: Multilateral development banks and similar institutions.
forms.why_use_it: Supranational bond issuer analysis. Used by fixed income investors who buy World Bank, ADB, or IADB bonds to assess the institution's financial health and portfolio quality.

forms.what: Quarterly report filed by multilateral development banks with bond programs in US markets.
forms.when: Quarterly per the institution's schedule.
forms.who: Multilateral development banks.
forms.why_use_it: Quarterly supranational bond data ΓÇö update on lending activity, capital adequacy, and funding needs of development finance institutions.

forms.full_name: Annual Audited Financial Report ΓÇô Broker-Dealers
forms.what: Annual FOCUS report containing audited financial statements, supporting net capital schedules, and financial condition data for registered broker-dealers.
forms.when: Within 60ΓÇô90 days after fiscal year-end.
forms.who: All registered broker-dealers.
forms.why_use_it: Broker-dealer financial health tracking. Used by clearing firms, prime brokers, and counterparties to assess the financial stability of broker-dealers they trade with.

forms.what: Registration and ongoing disclosure form for Alternative Trading Systems (ATS/dark pools). Discloses operational details of the dark pool, fee structure, subscriber information, and volume statistics.
forms.when: Initial: before commencing ATS operations. Amendments when material changes occur.
forms.who: Operators of Alternative Trading Systems (dark pools, ECNs, crossing networks).
forms.why_use_it: Dark pool transparency. ATS-N filings reveal who operates each dark pool, their subscriber list, order types, and trading volumes ΓÇö used by market structure researchers and regulators.

forms.what: Registration form for municipal advisors ΓÇö entities that advise municipalities on debt issuances. Requires registration with both SEC and MSRB.
forms.when: Before engaging in municipal advisory activities.
forms.who: Financial advisors, consultants, and banks that advise municipalities on securities issuance.
forms.why_use_it: Municipal bond market oversight. Tracks all registered municipal advisors ΓÇö relevant for investors in municipal bonds who want to understand the advisory ecosystem behind a bond issuance.

forms.what: Annual update to municipal advisor registration.
forms.when: Annually within 90 days of fiscal year-end.
forms.who: Registered municipal advisors.
forms.why_use_it: Maintains current information on municipal advisors ΓÇö staffing, disciplinary history, and any changes to advisory services.

forms.full_name: Municipal Advisor Registration ΓÇô Natural Persons
forms.what: Individual-level registration for natural persons (specific employees) associated with a municipal advisory firm.
forms.when: Before the individual engages in municipal advisory activities.
forms.who: Individual municipal advisor representatives.
forms.why_use_it: Individual advisor accountability ΓÇö tracks specific persons managing municipal debt advisory relationships.

forms.what: Short-form registration of securities on a national exchange (NYSE, NASDAQ) for companies already subject to Exchange Act reporting. Often filed alongside an S-1 or other registration at IPO.
forms.when: Before or upon listing on a national exchange.
forms.who: Already-reporting companies listing new securities classes on exchanges.
forms.why_use_it: Tracks exact moment securities become exchange-listed ΓÇö important for calculating official trading start dates and listing history.

forms.what: SEC-published notice on EDGAR that a registration statement (S-1, S-3, F-1, etc.) has been declared effective. The company may now proceed with its offering.
forms.when: Published by SEC when registration becomes effective.
forms.who: Published by SEC Division of Corporation Finance ΓÇö not filed by company.
forms.why_use_it: IPO green light signal. When an EFFECT notice appears on EDGAR, the IPO/offering is officially approved and trading can begin immediately. IPO arbitrageurs watch for EFFECT notices in real time.

forms.what: Filed when a company cannot file its 10-K annual report on time. Must explain the reason for the delay. Grants an automatic 15-calendar-day extension. Reasons: accounting complexities, auditor disputes, restatements, M&A due diligence.
forms.when: Filed by the original 10-K due date.
forms.who: Any registrant unable to meet its 10-K filing deadline.
forms.why_use_it: The earliest warning sign of accounting problems. Many fraud cases were preceded by NT 10-K filings. MarketBeat and Seeking Alpha immediately alert investors to NT 10-K filings. Stocks routinely sell off 3ΓÇô10% on NT 10-K news.

forms.what: Annual disclosure of whether a company's manufactured products contain conflict minerals (gold, tin, tungsten, tantalum) from the Democratic Republic of Congo or adjoining countries. Requires supply chain due diligence and independent audit if conflict minerals are present.
forms.when: Annually by May 31 for prior calendar year.
forms.who: SEC-reporting manufacturers whose products use conflict minerals necessary to production or functionality.
forms.why_use_it: ESG supply chain transparency. Impact investors and ESG raters use SD filings to score companies on conflict minerals sourcing practices. Tech companies (consumer electronics, semiconductors) file extensively.

forms.what: Annual disclosure of certain transactions with Iran that are sanctionable under the Iran Sanctions Act. Required when a company or affiliate engages in specified activities related to Iran's energy sector.
forms.when: Annually as part of periodic Exchange Act reports.
forms.who: Exchange Act reporting companies with activities subject to Iran Sanctions Act disclosure.
forms.why_use_it: Sanctions compliance monitoring. Institutional investors use IRANNOTICE disclosures in sanctions screening to identify potential divestment obligations or regulatory risk.

forms.what: Correspondence between the SEC staff and a registrant during review of a filing. Includes the SEC's comment letters (questions about disclosures) and the company's response letters. Made public after review is complete.
forms.when: Filed as correspondence is exchanged during SEC review process.
forms.who: Registrants engaged in active SEC filing reviews.
forms.why_use_it: Deep-dive analyst tool. SEC comment letters reveal exactly what regulators questioned ΓÇö revenue recognition, off-balance-sheet items, segment reporting, related-party transactions. Lawyers and forensic accountants read CORRESP filings to identify disclosure weaknesses.

forms.what: Request filed by a company or individual asking the SEC staff to issue a 'no-action' letter confirming they won't recommend enforcement action if a proposed transaction or practice proceeds as described.
forms.when: Filed as needed before a proposed action.
forms.who: Companies, law firms, or individuals seeking SEC guidance on novel or uncertain regulatory questions.
forms.why_use_it: Regulatory precedent research. No-action letters define the boundaries of securities law in practice ΓÇö used by securities lawyers and compliance officers to understand what the SEC will and won't pursue.

forms.what: Notice of proposed sale of restricted or control securities under SEC Rule 144. Filed by insiders or holders of restricted stock who plan to sell shares in the public market. Signals impending insider selling before it appears in Form 4.
forms.when: Filed when selling 5,000+ shares or securities worth $50,000+ in a 3-month period.
forms.who: Only affiliates (corporate insiders) proposing to sell securities under Rule 144.
forms.why_use_it: Pre-Form 4 selling signal. A Rule 144 notice gives a few days' advance warning that an insider is about to sell shares. Used by traders who monitor insider selling activity. Pre-dates the actual Form 4 disclosure of the completed sale.

forms.what: Exemption filing for foreign private issuers whose American Depositary Receipts trade in US markets without full SEC registration (OTC/Pink Sheet ADRs).
forms.when: Filed when FPI establishes an informal ADR program.
forms.who: Foreign private issuers with OTC ADR programs not requiring full SEC registration.
forms.why_use_it: Tracks the universe of Level 1 ADRs and OTC-traded foreign securities ΓÇö important for international stock databases tracking available ADR investment opportunities.

forms.what: Registration statement for entities seeking to operate as SEC-registered crowdfunding funding portals under Regulation Crowdfunding.
forms.when: Before commencing crowdfunding portal operations.
forms.who: Entities seeking to operate Regulation CF funding portals.
forms.why_use_it: Crowdfunding platform registry ΓÇö tracks all SEC-registered portals (Republic, Wefunder, StartEngine). Used by investors to verify a portal's regulatory standing before investing through it.

forms.what: Certification by the New York Stock Exchange confirming a company meets NYSE continued listing standards.
forms.when: Filed annually as part of NYSE listing compliance.
forms.who: NYSE-listed companies certifying continued compliance with listing standards.
forms.why_use_it: Listing standards compliance confirmation. A missed CERTNYS or deficiency notice signals a company may be at risk of NYSE delisting.

forms.what: Draft registration statement submitted confidentially to the SEC for review before public filing ΓÇö allows companies to test the IPO process and get SEC comments without public disclosure. JOBS Act creation.
forms.when: Submitted confidentially; must be publicly filed at least 15 days before IPO roadshow.
forms.who: EGCs (Emerging Growth Companies) and FPIs confidentially submitting IPO registration statements.
forms.why_use_it: Confidential IPO pipeline tracking. When a DRS becomes public (DRSLTR or public S-1), it confirms a company is actively in the IPO process. Investors use public DRS disclosures to start analyzing upcoming IPOs early.
